| `RESCUES` | Dramatic single-moment extraction from danger — rescuer saves the target from death, capture, or peril. Distinct from `PROTECTS` (steady-state guardianship) and `HEALS` (medical restoration). Covers Beric rescuing BWB members, Davos rescued by Salladhor, Sam & Gilly rescued by the Mysterious Rider, Jon rescuing Ghost from wildlings. Tier-3 (no qualifier). | Rescuer → Rescued-person | corpus |
| `TORTURES` | The act of inflicting deliberate physical pain — flaying, racking, burning, or other sustained bodily torment. Distinct from `ATTACKS` (generic physical violence; not necessarily sustained or systematic), `ASSAULTS` (sexual violence specifically), `EXECUTES` (formal death sentence), and the `REVEALS_TO=under_torture` qualifier (which captures the informational *effect* of torture, not the act itself). Major narrative thread: Bolton flaying tradition, Mountain at Harrenhal, Qyburn's "studies", the Bloody Mummers, Black Cells. Without this edge, "who has Ramsay tortured?" cannot be graph-answered. Tier-3 (no qualifier; method is sub-typology the narrative captures). | Torturer → Tortured-person | corpus |
| `AGENT_IN` | Acts as the agent/executor of an event — the participant who actually performed the act. Source is the executor; target is the event node. Reification role edge: use when attaching a character or house to a multi-party event hub instead of (or in addition to) a dyadic binary edge. See `VICTIM_IN` (patient), `COMMANDS_IN` (orderer who did not personally execute), `WIELDED_IN` (instrument). | Person/House → Event (`event.*`) | — |
| `VICTIM_IN` | Receives the action of an event as the victim or patient — the participant on whom the act was performed. Source is the recipient; target is the event node. Reification role edge: complement to `AGENT_IN` on the same event hub. | Person/House → Event (`event.*`) | — |
| `WITNESS_IN` | The **observer/perceiver** slot on an event hub — a character whose *load-bearing perception* of a charged event (violence, death, a secret, an atrocity) is itself worth a graph edge, distinct from acting (`AGENT_IN`), suffering (`VICTIM_IN`), ordering (`COMMANDS_IN`), or combatting (`FIGHTS_IN`). **Default to an EDGE** onto the existing event node (the Q2 three-lens consensus, S117): reify the witnessing as its own `event.*` NODE *only* when the act of seeing owns an outgoing causal edge — i.e. the seeing `CAUSES`/`TRIGGERS`/`MOTIVATES` a downstream event (e.g. `bran-witnesses-jaime-and-cersei`, whose seeing causes his fall) — or the witnessed thing has no node of its own. Mechanical test: *does the seeing need an outgoing edge? yes → node; no → edge.* Promotion path: start as an edge, promote to a node later only if a causal edge actually gets drawn. **Text-anchor gate:** emit only when the prose shows the character actually SEES it — present-but-shielded does NOT qualify (Yoren forces Arya's eyes shut at Ned's execution → Arya is NOT a witness; Sansa, who "could not turn her head," IS). **Boundary vs `ATTENDS`:** `ATTENDS` = voluntary audience at a *staged social/ceremonial gathering* (feast, wedding, tourney-as-a-whole, court, coronation); `WITNESS_IN` = perception of a charged/violent/secret incident. Same person can `ATTENDS` the Tourney of the Hand AND `WITNESS_IN` the Loras-vs-Gregor bout within it (different targets). Tier-1 when verbatim-cited. Added Session 117 (2026-06-21); first instances `sansa-stark WITNESS_IN execution-of-eddard-stark`, `arianne-martell WITNESS_IN myrcella-is-maimed-by-darkstar`, `hoster-tully WITNESS_IN battle-of-the-camps`. | Person → Event (`event.*`) | — |

> **Reification role vocabulary note:** `WIELDED_IN` (already in the Possession & Artifacts section, artifact→event) serves the **instrument** role for reified events — no `INSTRUMENT_IN` type is needed. `COMMANDS_IN` (below) serves the **orderer/instigator** role. Together with `AGENT_IN` and `VICTIM_IN`, these four cover the standard **action/instrument** participant slots (agent, patient, orderer, instrument) on an event hub. The **perceiver/observer** slot is a fifth role, `WITNESS_IN` (S117) — epistemic, not action (the perceiver acts on nothing and receives nothing); voluntary audience at a *staged* gathering is `ATTENDS`, not `WITNESS_IN`.

### Knowledge & Information

> **Deprecated (Session 63, 2026-05-21):** `KNOWS` was removed from the active vocabulary. Stage 4 wiki-prose classification ran an 82.3% fallback rate on KNOWS emits — the semantic boundary ("knows of" vs "met once" vs "heard rumor of") is too blurry for prose-derived classification to enforce. Character-knowledge relationships will be derived from a future Pass-1-based chapter co-occurrence + Information Revealed pass. Existing KNOWS edges in the graph (363 Haiku-emit + 21 Sonnet-control-arm) are preserved as historical record; downstream consumers filter deprecated types on read.

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `IGNORANT_OF` | Critically lacks information that the reader or other characters have | Person → Information | — |
| `SEEKS` | Pursuing knowledge, artifact, or person | Seeker → Sought | — |
| `REVEALS_TO` | Discloses information to another | Revealer → Recipient (note what was revealed) | — |
| `DECEIVES` | Deliberately misleads | Deceiver → Deceived (note the deception) | — |
| `DECEIVED_BY` | Reverse of `DECEIVES` — emitted on target nodes pointing to deceiver | Deceived → Deceiver | — |
| `HOARDS` | Institution or person suppresses knowledge | Hoarder → Knowledge | — |
| `INVESTIGATES` | Actively trying to learn or prove something | Investigator → Subject | — |
| `TEACHES` | Transmits knowledge or skill (general/casual instruction) | Teacher → Student | — |
| `TUTORS` | Sustained formal one-on-one mentorship — narrower than `TEACHES` (Syrio→Arya water-dancing, Cressen→Stannis childhood, Aemon→Sam ravenry, Septa Mordane→Sansa) | Tutor → Student | — |
| `HEALS` | Medical or maester treatment — restoration of body, not resurrection of the dead (which is `RESURRECTS`). Maester Luwin healing Bran after the fall; Aemon healing Sam; the unnamed septon healing Sandor. **Excludes:** Red Priests reviving the dead (use `RESURRECTS`); Qyburn's reanimation of the Mountain (use `RESURRECTS`) | Healer → Healed | — |
| `AFFLICTED_BY` | Character suffers from a named disease, condition, or magical affliction (living state). Target is `concept.medical`. Distinct from `KILLED_BY` (target = person), `DIED_AT` (location), `DIED_OF` (cause-of-death; this is the living state). Examples: Jorah Mormont/greyscale, Shireen Baratheon/greyscale, Stannis/burns. | Character → Medical | — |
| `DIED_OF` | Character's death was caused by a named disease/condition (post-mortem state). Target is `concept.medical`. Distinct from `KILLED_BY` (person-killer), `DIED_AT` (location), `EXECUTED_WITH` (judicial weapon). Mirrors `AFFLICTED_BY` for the post-mortem state. Examples: Hoster Tully/Spring Sickness, Albin Massey/Shivers, Medrick Manderly/Winter Fever, the Old King Jaehaerys/Great Spring Sickness. | Character → Medical | — |
| `SPIES_ON` | A spy or agent actively surveils, monitors, or gathers intelligence about the target. Distinct from `SERVES` (employment state) and `MANIPULATES` (wrong direction — spy works for handler, not against target). Westeros has structurally-distinct spy networks (Varys's little birds, Littlefinger's network, Qarth's whispers). Tier-3. | Person → Surveilled-person | n/a (Pass 1 corpus + Sonnet no-fit) |
| `INFORMS` | A spy, agent, or informant reports intelligence to their handler or spymaster. Complements `SPIES_ON` (target = person being watched vs. target = handler receiving reports). Distinct from `SERVES` (too generic) and `REVEALS_TO` (one-time disclosure; INFORMS is the ongoing reporting relationship). Tier-3. | Person → Handler/Spymaster | n/a (Pass 1 corpus + Sonnet no-fit) |

### Emotional & Perceptual

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `PERCEIVED_AS` | How POV Character X sees Character Y (with characterization notes) | Perceiver → Perceived | — |
| `TRUSTS` | Places confidence in | Truster → Trusted | — |
| `DISTRUSTS` | Lacks confidence in, suspects | Distruster → Distrusted | — |
| `RESPECTS` | Holds in high regard | Respecter → Respected | — |
| `FEARS` | Afraid of (person, faction, or thing) | Fearer → Feared | — |
| `LOVES` | Deep emotional attachment (familial, romantic, or platonic) | Lover → Loved | — |
| `HATES` | Deep enmity or loathing | Hater → Hated | — |
| `MOURNS` | Grieves for (dead person, lost thing) | Mourner → Mourned | — |
| `PROTECTS` | Acts as guardian or defender | Protector → Protected | — |
| `RESENTS` | Harbors bitterness toward | Resenter → Resented | — |
| `COMPANION_OF` | Close personal friendship or camaraderie. Distinct from `ALLIES_WITH` (political alliance), `TRUSTS` (one-direction confidence), `LOVES` (romantic/deep-familial), `RESPECTS` (cold regard). Use when prose explicitly names a friendship ("good friends with", "sworn brothers", "close companion"). Examples: Patrek Mallister & Edmure Tully; Robert & Ned in their youth; Brienne & Pod; Davos & Salladhor Saan. | Symmetric | — |
| `REPUTED_AS` | Collective reputation or general perception attached to a character without a specific perceiver — distinct from `PERCEIVED_AS` which requires a named POV. Target is `concept.*` (e.g., `concept.magic` for "reputed witch", `concept.craft` for "reputed swordsman", `concept.vice` for "reputed drunkard"). Use when prose narrates a public-domain reputation rather than one character's view of another. | Character → Concept | — |
| `ENCOUNTERS` | Plot-significant face-to-face meeting between two characters, anchored by explicit prose staging (verb gate enforced by validator per CRITICAL RULE — see classify prompt Rule 6). Reserved for first meetings, brief road-crossings, set-piece confrontations short of duel/combat where the meeting itself carries narrative weight. Examples: Dunk ↔ Egg at the Ashford inn; Brienne ↔ Randyll Tarly in AFFC; Arya ↔ Sandor on the kingsroad; Jon ↔ Mance at the Frostfangs; Sam ↔ Coldhands beyond the Wall. NOT for co-presence at events (use `LOCATED_AT` / `ATTENDS` / `FIGHTS_IN`), retinue/court presence (`TRAVELS_WITH`), or formal diplomatic engagement (`NEGOTIATES_WITH`). **Coverage scope (Session 63, 2026-05-21):** Stage 4 captures only wiki-prose-staged meetings — biographical-summary register often elides staging verbs even when meetings happened in-text. Comprehensive character-meeting coverage will come from a future book-derived pass; treat wiki ENCOUNTERS as partial-by-design. Tier-3 (no qualifier). | Symmetric (character ↔ character) | — |

### Spatial & Temporal

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `LOCATED_AT` | Entity at location (with book/chapter timestamp). Covers both event-at-place (battle location) and person-at-place (witness location). Deprecated synonym `LOCATED_IN` was emitted by an early parser variant; normalize on read. | Entity → Location | Location, Seat |
| `SEAT_OF` | Primary location of a house or faction | Location → House/Faction | Seat, Seats |
| `TRAVELS_TO` | Movement from one location to another | Traveler → Destination (note origin) | — |
| `TRAVELS_WITH` | Co-presence in someone's company, on the road OR in attendance at court/event. Covers both journeying together (Arya/Gendry/Hot Pie; Sam+Gilly+Aemon to Oldtown; Dunk+Egg) AND retinue/court presence (Robett kneeling among Catelyn's welcomers at Winterfell). Distinct from `COMPANION_OF` (sustained friendship), `ALLIES_WITH` (political), `MEMBER_OF` (faction affiliation), `SERVES` (subordination). Tier-3 (no qualifier). | Symmetric (character ↔ character) | — |
| `BORN_AT` | Birthplace | Person → Location | Born |
| `DIED_AT` | Place of death | Person → Location | Died |
| `BURIED_AT` | Place of burial or interment | Person → Location | Buried |
| `IMPRISONED_AT` | Captive's place of confinement during a specific imprisonment (cell, dungeon, sky cell, black cells, tower used as gaol, prison hulk). Distinct from `LOCATED_AT` (general presence — not captivity-marked), `IMPRISONS` (jailer→captive judicial relationship), and `PRISONER_OF` (captive↔captor symmetric state). Examples: Ned/Black Cells; Tyrion/Eyrie sky cell; Jaime/Robb's camp at Riverrun; Davos/Dragonstone dungeon. Tier-3 (no qualifier). | Captive → Location (`place.location`) | — |
| `CONTEMPORARY_WITH` | Events happen simultaneously or overlap in time | Symmetric | — |
| `REGION_OF` | Location belongs to a larger geographic region | Location → Region | Region, Regions |

### Possession & Ownership

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `WIELDS` | Currently bears or uses a weapon/artifact. **Target MUST be `object.artifact`** — animals (mounts, ravens, dragons) use `OWNS` or `BONDED_TO`, never `WIELDS`. | Person → Artifact | — |
| `OWNS` | Possesses (broader than wields — castles, ships, animals) | Owner → Owned | Owner, Owners |
| `ANCESTRAL_WEAPON_OF` | Valyrian steel sword or other hereditary weapon of a house | Weapon → House | Ancestral weapon |
| `FORGED_BY` | Creator/smith of an artifact (the person/group who made it). **NOT for material composition** — use `MADE_OF` for substance/material relationships. | Creator → Artifact | — |
| `MADE_OF` | Artifact is composed of a material (Valyrian steel, dragonglass, dragonbone, weirwood, etc.). Distinct from `FORGED_BY` (smith). | Artifact → Material (`object.material`) | — |
| `LOOTED_BY` | Artifact taken by force or conquest from prior holder. Distinct from `OWNS` (steady state). Captures the transactional moment. | Artifact → New holder | — |
| `REFORGED_INTO` | Original artifact materially transformed into a new artifact (or multiple). The original ceases to exist; the new artifact(s) inherit material and lineage. | Original artifact → Resulting artifact | — |
| `GIFTED_TO` | Deliberate voluntary transfer of an artifact from one person to another as gift or honor. Distinct from `OWNS` (state) and `INHERITED_BY` (death-succession). Note giver in qualifier. | Artifact → Recipient | — |
| `INHERITED_BY` | Artifact passed via inheritance from deceased holder to heir. | Artifact → Heir | — |
| `WIELDED_IN` | Artifact was used in a named event (battle, execution, ritual). Distinct from `WIELDS` (person → artifact possession state). Enables artifact-history queries. | Artifact → Event | — |
| `EXECUTED_WITH` | A specific person was executed with a specific weapon (poetic-detail edges: Eddard executed with Ice, etc.). May overlap with `WIELDED_IN` + `EXECUTES`; kept distinct for narrative-precision queries. | Victim → Weapon | — |
| `PURCHASED_FROM` | Transactional acquisition of an artifact (or service) via purchase — distinct from `OWNS` (steady state), `GIFTED_TO` (voluntary transfer), `LOOTED_BY` (taken by force), `INHERITED_BY` (death-succession). Captures the transactional moment + the seller. Examples: Dunk purchasing the dragon-of-Pentos shield from Pate the Old; ship-passage purchases (when concretely named); merchant exchanges that are plot-significant. | Buyer → Seller | — |
| `BUILT` | Character physically built or oversaw the construction of a named structure (castle, tower, sept, wall, monument). Distinct from `FOUNDED` (scoped to organizations/houses/orders) and `OWNS` (steady state). Use when prose explicitly names the builder of a place. Examples: Brandon-the-Builder/The Wall, Brandon-the-Builder/Storm's End (legend), Lord-Triston-Hightower/Starry-Sept. | Builder → Structure (`place.location`) | — |
| `CAPTAIN_OF` | Character is captain (master/commander) of a named vessel. Target MUST be `object.artifact` (the vessel). Distinct from `COMMANDS` (military org) and `OWNS` (ownership state — captains may or may not own the vessel). Examples: Davos Seaworth/Black Betha, Victarion Greyjoy/Iron Victory, Asha Greyjoy/Black Wind, Salladhor Saan/Valyrian. | Captain → Vessel (`object.artifact`) | — |
| `CREW_OF` | Character serves as a crew member (non-captain) of a named vessel — sibling to `CAPTAIN_OF`. Target MUST be `object.artifact` (the vessel). Use when prose explicitly names a non-captain role (oarsman, first mate, ship's cook, sail-master). Captain → use `CAPTAIN_OF`. Generic faction membership → use `MEMBER_OF`. Note specific role in `notes` if known ("first mate"). | Crew member → Vessel (`object.artifact`) | — |

### Identity & Disguise

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `ALIAS_OF` | Known by another name. **Substitution test (canonical rule, S86 2026-06-08):** two strings are aliases iff substituting one for the other in any sentence about the entity does NOT change the truth value. "Wedding at the Twins" ↔ "Red Wedding" passes — same event. "Lord Walder calls for the bedding" ↔ "Red Wedding" FAILS — the bedding-call is a moment WITHIN the Red Wedding, not the whole event. Granularity differences are NOT aliases; emit `SUB_BEAT_OF` instead. The dashes-vs-spaces test catches surface variants only, NOT granularity. | Alias → True Identity | Alias, Aliases |
| `DISGUISED_AS` | Actively pretending to be someone/something else | Person → Disguise Identity | — |
| `SAME_AS` | Two references that resolve to the same entity (for cross-identity matching) | Symmetric | — |
| `IMPERSONATES` | Pretending to be a specific other person | Impersonator → Impersonated | — |

### Magic & Supernatural

> These edges capture relationships that involve magical, ritual, or supernatural agency — distinct from in-world physical relations. ASOIAF has rich magical systems (warging, R'hllor blood magic, Faceless-Men identity-transfer, weirwood-bonding, dragon-bonding, resurrection, curse-laying) that infobox extraction cannot reach. These types are *prose-derived only* — the wiki-infobox parser does not emit them.

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `WARGS_INTO` | A warg / skinchanger actively occupies the consciousness of an animal or person — e.g., Bran into Summer / Hodor / the heart tree; Arya into Nymeria (dream-skinchange); Varamyr into his wolf / eagle / shadowcat; Orell into his eagle; Jon (low-key) into Ghost | Warg → Vessel | — |
| `BONDED_TO` | Static magical bond between two beings — broader and more permanent than `WARGS_INTO` (which is the active occupation moment). Covers dragon-rider bonds (Daenerys ↔ Drogon, etc.), warg-animal lifelong pairing (Bran ↔ Summer when not actively warging), weirwood-bond (Bran ↔ his three-eyed-crow / weirwood network) | Symmetric | — |
| `SACRIFICES` | Deliberate ritual or magical killing with supernatural/symbolic purpose — distinct from `KILLS` (combat) and `EXECUTES` (judicial). Mirri Maz Duur sacrificing Drogo's life-essence; Daenerys sacrificing her unborn child to magic the dragon eggs; Stannis (via Melisandre) sacrificing Edric Storm's leech-blood / Mance / Penny's brother / (theory-tier) Shireen; Craster sacrificing his sons to the Others | Sacrificer → Victim | — |
| `RESURRECTS` | Returns the dead to life via supernatural means — distinct from `HEALS` (medical), distinct from `KILLED_BY` (semantic reverse). Thoros of Myr resurrects Beric Dondarrion (multiple times); Beric resurrects Catelyn → Lady Stoneheart (ASOS Epilogue); Coldhands resurrected by unknown force (Children?); Patchface drowned-and-returned; Qyburn reanimates the Mountain; Red Priests broadly perform this (Thoros, Moqorro) — `HEALS` is for body-restoration, `RESURRECTS` is for death-reversal | Resurrector → Resurrected | — |
| `CURSES` | A character or magical force lays a curse — Mirri Maz Duur's "when the sun rises in the west" curse on Daenerys; Maggy the Frog's Valonqar prophecy-curse on Cersei; the Curse of Harrenhal (collective); Night's King lore | Curser → Cursed | — |
| `PRACTICES` | Character actively practices a named magical or ritual discipline. Target is `concept.magic` (or `concept.craft` for non-magical-but-named-discipline cases). Distinct from `WARGS_INTO` (active occupation moment), `BONDED_TO` (static pairing), `WORSHIPS` (religious devotion, not the magical practice itself), `CLERGY_OF` (religious office). Examples: Melisandre/shadow-binding, Mirri Maz Duur/maegi-blood-magic, Bran/greendreams, faceless-men/identity-transfer, Qyburn/necromancy. | Character → Magic discipline | — |

### Cultural & Religious

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `CULTURE_OF` | Person belongs to a cultural group | Person → Culture | Culture, Race |
| `WORSHIPS` | Follows or serves a deity/religion | Person → Religion | Religion |
| `SACRED_TO` | Location or artifact is holy to a religion | Entity → Religion | — |
| `CLERGY_OF` | Serves as religious official | Person → Religion | — |
| `OFFICIATES` | Character performs the ritual / religious / ceremonial role at a named event (weddings, funerals, coronations, kingsmoots, namedays, knighting ceremonies). Distinct from `CLERGY_OF` (general clergy status, target = religion) and `ATTENDS` (guest/audience). Target is `event.*` or specific named ceremony node. Examples: Melisandre/wedding-of-sigorn-and-alys-karstark; the High Septon/coronation-of-tommen-i; Aeron Damphair/kingsmoot-of-299-ac. | Character → Event | — |
| `HONORED_AT` | Character is the **ceremonial honoree** — the recipient of a conferred honor at a named event (queen of love and beauty crowned at a tourney, a nameday honoree, the dubbed knight at a knighting). The reification-role complement on a ceremonial event hub: the **conferrer** uses `AGENT_IN` (e.g. Rhaegar laying the laurel), the **honoree** uses `HONORED_AT`. Distinct from `ATTENDS` (audience), `OFFICIATES` (performs the rite), `WITNESS_IN` (perceiver), `AGENT_IN` (the one who acts/confers). Added Session 134 (2026-06-23) to retire the off-vocab `CROWNS_QUEEN_OF_LOVE_AND_BEAUTY` tail-LLM leakage; first instance `lyanna-stark HONORED_AT crowning-of-lyanna-at-harrenhal`. Tier-1 when verbatim-cited; no qualifier. | Character → Event (`event.*`) | — |
| `NAMED_AFTER` | An entity was given its name in honor of, or as a reference to, another entity. Captures Westeros's pervasive dynastic name-recycling culture (Rickard Karstark named for Rickard Stark, the many Brandons and Aegons). Distinct from `ALIAS_OF` (a different name for the same entity), `SAME_AS` (identity-resolution), and `DEPICTED_IN` (in-world legend about a person). One-sided: the named entity points to its namesake. Surfaced in Sonnet `no-fitting-type` rejections. Tier-3 (no qualifier). | Entity → Namesake-entity | wiki (dynastic naming patterns) |

### Narrative & Literary

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `DEPICTED_IN` | Character is the subject of an in-world text/song/ballad/tale (Danny Flint → "Brave Danny Flint" song, Florian → Florian-and-Jonquil tales). Distinct from `WRITTEN_BY` (author → work). Captures the in-universe legacy/folklore layer. | Character → Text | — |

*continued:*

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `FORESHADOWS` | Detail A is a Chekhov's gun for Event B | Detail → Event | — |
| `PARALLELS` | Event/character A mirrors Event/character B thematically | Symmetric | — |
| `SUBVERTS` | Event A inverts the expectation set by B | Subverter → Subverted | — |
| `ECHOES` | Weaker than PARALLELS — structural or verbal similarity without full thematic mirroring | Echo → Source | — |
| `CONTRASTS` | Deliberate opposition in characterization, situation, or outcome | Symmetric | — |
| `WRITTEN_BY` | Authorship of an in-world text (book, song, decree, letter) | Text → Author | Written by |

### Prophecy

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `FULFILLS` | Event fulfills prophecy (confirmed) | Event → Prophecy | — |
| `APPEARS_TO_FULFILL` | Possible fulfillment, may be red herring | Event → Prophecy | — |
| `SUBVERTS_PROPHECY` | Contradicts expected fulfillment | Event → Prophecy | — |
| `PROPHESIED_BY` | Who made the prophecy | Prophecy → Prophet | — |
| `SUBJECT_OF_PROPHECY` | Person/event the prophecy is about | Person → Prophecy | — |
| `DREAMS_OF` | In-world prophetic or significant dream/vision about a person, place, event, or symbol. Distinct from `FORESHADOWS` (which is a reader-facing narrative-craft edge); `DREAMS_OF` is character-facing. Heavily concentrated in Bran (greendreams, three-eyed-crow), Daenerys (HotU visions), Jojen, and the Targaryen line (canonical prophetic-dreaming lineage) | Dreamer → Subject | — |

### Evidentiary (Theory Support)

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `SUPPORTS` | A real passage the theory community reads as evidence FOR a theory — the edge asserts the reading exists, not that the theory is true. Every edge carries its own byte-verified verbatim quote + chapter:line ref | Evidence node → Theory | — |
| `CONTRADICTS` | A real passage that undermines a theory (or one scoped sub-claim — scope lives in the edge `note`) | Evidence node → Theory | — |
| `CITED_BY` | DEFERRED (S216) — no out-of-world source node type; provenance lives in theory-node frontmatter (`video_sources`, `origin`) | Theory → Source | — |

**Theory-layer conventions (ratified S216–S217, Matt):**

- **Tier floor is absolute:** theory nodes and SUPPORTS/CONTRADICTS edges are **tier 3–5 ALWAYS** — no "event-anchored" or other exceptions. Per-edge tier variance under a node is allowed (a tier-3 node may carry tier-4/5 edges).
- **Layer rule:** facts already owned by tier-1/2 nodes are the theory's *premise* (prose), not theory evidence — don't re-mint them as edges. An existing node CAN source an edge where its confirmed reality bears directly on the claim (the subject-link pattern).
- **Subject-link pattern:** a theory may link to the event/artifact node it is *about* via a scoped evidence edge (GNC G1 precedent: the confirmed event node sources a SUPPORTS into the theory that generalizes it).
- **Parallel-FORESHADOWS (S217):** when a theory-evidence quote foreshadows an event that actually occurs on the page, the factual layer gets a `FORESHADOWS` edge to that event node (canon-visible; tier 2–4 by mapping strength, typically 2–3) alongside the theory-layer SUPPORTS edge. Both edges may carry the same quote — different layers, different claims.
- **Naming:** display names are claim-style ("Roose Bolton is a skin-stealing immortal"); genuinely unresolved multi-candidate whodunits use question-form ("Who is Coldhands?", "Who Wrote the Pink Letter?", "Who is the Hooded Man of Winterfell?").
- **One node per canonical named theory;** umbrella wiki "X/Theories" stubs stay dark unless they ARE the canonical theory.
- **Evidence sources:** any node class whose entity the quote is about may source an evidence edge — including `object.food` (weirwood-paste), legend/`object.text` (rat-cook), and `concept` nodes (wights). From wave 2 onward, theory edges may cite `fab-*` (Fire & Blood) chapters — same quotecheck machinery; chronicler unreliability handled by tier + `in_universe_source`.
- **Ungrounded material** (show-only, TWOW/TWOIAF-fenced, GRRM interviews, community reasoning) is domain-labelled in the node body — never edges.
- **Chat exposure:** theories are invisible to the chat persona (SHARED_RULES guardrail). Plan of record (S217): an off-by-default UI toggle — OFF = runtime tool-layer filter strips theory nodes/edges; ON = labeled-speculation prompt contract. Build is a separate gated session.

### Causal & Plot

| Edge Type | Description | Directionality | Wiki Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| `CAUSES` | Event A leads to Event B | Cause → Effect | — |
| `PREVENTS` | Action A blocks Event B | Preventer → Prevented | — |
| `ENABLES` | Condition A makes Event B possible | Enabler → Enabled | — |
| `MOTIVATES` | Event or condition drives a character's actions | Motivation → Actor | — |
| `TRIGGERS` | Immediate cause (narrower than CAUSES — the specific spark) | Trigger → Result | — |
| `SUSPECTED_OF` | A character is suspected — in-world and/or by the reader — of being the agent or cause of an event, where the published text does NOT prove it. The unproven counterpart to `AGENT_IN`/`KILLS`/`CAUSES`: use when the suspicion is itself load-bearing but the act is unconfirmed in the 5 published books. **Capped at Tier-2 — NEVER Tier-1** (Tier-1 is reserved for proven canon; an asserted-as-fact killing uses `KILLS`/`AGENT_IN`). Cite the in-text basis (accusation, portents, timing) in the rationale; out-of-text corroboration (TWOW-preview, author statements) may be noted but does not raise the tier. Added Session 116 (2026-06-20) — first instance: `euron-greyjoy SUSPECTED_OF death-of-balon-greyjoy` (Asha's accusation "Silence returns within a day of my lord father's death" + the storm-portents; Euron's confession exists only in the *Forsaken* TWOW-preview chapter, not published canon, so the killing stays unproven and the node stays `event.death`, not `event.assassination`). | Suspect → Event (`event.*`) | — |

#### The `ENABLES` vs `CAUSES` vs `TRIGGERS` contract (S121, 2026-06-21)

The three forward-causal edges are NOT interchangeable; they form a strength ladder, and the choice is load-bearing for graph honesty (the "sequence-only trap" — drawing causation where only chronology exists). Pick by this rubric:

- **`TRIGGERS`** — A is the **immediate spark** of B; B is the very next beat, nothing decisional sits between them. *Is B the very next thing, or is there a step in between?* If next → TRIGGERS. (`ned-confesses-to-treason TRIGGERS execution-of-eddard-stark`.)

content.content:
1	# TMK — Dunk 1
2	
3	## Chapter Metadata
4	- **book:** TMK
5	- **chapter_number:** 1
6	- **pov_character:** Dunk
7	- **pov_chapter_number:** Dunk I
8	- **first_available:** pre-agot
9	- **location_primary:** Whitewalls (Lord Butterwell's castle — the tourney yard/lists, the wine cellar where Dunk is treated, the outer ward, the Milkhouse, and the well)
10	- **location_secondary:** The cellar/wine-store beneath the castle; the postern gate; (mentioned) Nunny, Bitterbridge, Misty Moor, the Twins / the Crossing, Ashford Meadow, the Reach, Winterfell, Summerhall, Asshai by the Shadow, Flea Bottom, Dorne, Pennytree, the Seven Kingdoms; (recalled) the ship White Lady
11	- **approximate_timeline:** Tournament morning, the day after the wedding feast; unit part 5 of 9. Sequence: the first-round jousts play out under punishing heat (Paege/Frey, Ball/Argrave, Caswell/Kyle); Dunk arms himself, dismisses his opponent, and is knocked senseless by Ser Uthor Underleaf's lance; he wakes four hours later in the cellar under a maester's care; he returns to the field to watch the Fiddler unhorse Franklyn Frey, passes the departing dwarfs, and at the well is urged by Maynard Plumm to flee Whitewalls, which he refuses.
12	- **time_markers:** "The sun rose hot and hard"; "this morning"; "last night" (the feast); "an hour before they called him forth again"; "Two more before my turn"; "You have not ridden in a tilt since Ashford Meadow"; "Ser Uthor is ten years my senior and half my size"; a "weedy youth of twenty" (Lord Caswell); "Four hours, by the sundial" (Dunk unconscious); tale of a knight who "slept for forty years"; "before the day is out"; "clouds ... gathering to the east"
13	- **chapter_summary:** On a blistering tournament morning at Whitewalls, Dunk watches the early tilts — Franklyn Frey unhorsing Harbert Paege, Glendon Ball unhorsing Ser Argrave, and Lord Joffrey Caswell toppling Ser Kyle the Cat, who threw the match hoping to be taken back into Caswell's service. Overconfident and hungover, Dunk dismisses his own opponent, the snail-sigiled Ser Uthor Underleaf, only to be struck square between the eyes by Uthor's lance and knocked from Thunder. He wakes four hours later in a wine cellar, tended by a maester, and learns he has lost his horse, arms, and armor to Uthor by the terms of the tilt. Returning dazed to the field, he sees the Fiddler unhorse Franklyn Frey and pass the departing troupe of dwarfs. At the well, Ser Maynard Plumm urges him to take his horse and armor and slip out the postern gate, but Dunk — his mount and gear now belonging to Uthor — refuses to leave a beggar or a thief, invoking the honor Ser Arlan taught him.
14	- **unit_part:** 5 of 9
15	
16	## Physical Environment
17	- **Weather:** Punishing heat — "The sun rose hot and hard, implacable"; "Waves of heat rose shimmering off the white stones"; no wind, "no breath of wind stirred the banners that drooped"; the sun "beat down on his head like a hammer on an anvil"; a few clouds "gathering to the east, dark as Dunk's mood"
18	- **Season indicators:** High summer / drought — "baked earth and torn grass," "Clods of cracked dry earth sprayed back from beneath their horses' hooves," "too hot for man or mount"
19	- **Time of day:** Morning through midday; the sun rising then beating down; "Four hours, by the sundial" pass while Dunk is unconscious
20	- **Lighting:** Bright, glaring sun off the white stones; the cellar is dim and cool ("At least it is cool here"); "the bright blue sky"
21	- **Sounds:** The crack and explosion of breaking lances; a cheer "short and thin and hollow"; a trumpet "loud enough to make Dunk wince"; "a noise ... part roar and part moan" when a rider falls; a "gale of laughter" at the herald's mockery; cheering "all around him"; Dunk's voice echoing off the cellar ceiling; his head "beating like a drum," skull "pounding like some monstrous war drum"
22	- **Smells:** The greathelm lining "stank of old sweat"; the ugliest dwarf "smelled like a privy"; (recalled) baked earth
23	- **Notable sensory details:** Heat-shimmer off white stone; the drooping green, white and yellow banners; the taste of blood in Dunk's mouth on waking; the "foul potion"; the barrel-vaulted cellar ceiling and casks of wine and ale; a hawk/herald soaring in "wide circles" that Dunk envies
24

29	| Egg | Dunk's squire; saddles Thunder, hands up helm and shield and lance, urges Dunk to withdraw | (uncertain — verify) | Shaved head; wears a floppy straw hat; sits with the injured Dunk until the maester chases him off |
30	| Ser Uthor Underleaf (the Snail) | Dunk's opponent; unhorses him with a blow between the eyes; wins his next tilt too | Yes | Ten years Dunk's senior, half his size; green enamel plate, silver snail on green shield; quick, good seat |
31	| Ser Harbert Paege | Jousts Ser Franklyn Frey; is unhorsed | Yes | Golden courser barded in black; red and white serpents of House Paege |

42	| Ser Maynard Plumm | At the well with Kyle; urges Dunk to flee Whitewalls; predicts the Fiddler will win the egg | (uncertain — verify) | Did not joust ("the omens had gone sour"); warns Whitewalls is unhealthy for Egg |

You won't be missed" |

147	## Events & Actions
148	1. **The early tilts run under the heat** — Paege and Frey break four pairs of lances before a hollow cheer; the field bakes.
149	   - Agent: Ser Harbert Paege; Ser Franklyn Frey
150	   - Location: the lists at Whitewalls
151	   - Outcome: repeated broken lances, no unhorsing (yet)
152	2. **Ser Franklyn Frey unhorses Ser Harbert Paege** — after many passes, "the golden horse trotting riderless ... as Ser Harbert Paege rolled feebly on the ground."
153	   - Agent: Ser Franklyn Frey
154	   - Patient: Ser Harbert Paege
155	   - Instrument: tourney lance
156	   - Location: the lists
157	   - Outcome: Paege unhorsed
158	3. **Glendon Ball unhorses Ser Argrave the Defiant** — "Ball's coronal found the center of his foe's breastplate with such force that it burst his saddle cinch. Knight and saddle both went tumbling to the dust."
159	   - Agent: Glendon Ball
160	   - Patient: Ser Argrave the Defiant
161	   - Instrument: tourney lance (coronal)
162	   - Location: the lists
163	   - Witness: Dunk ("Dunk was impressed despite himself")
164	   - Outcome: Argrave unhorsed, saddle burst
165	4. **Lord Joffrey Caswell unhorses Ser Kyle the Cat** — Kyle throws the match; "the Cat went tumbling when Lord Joffrey's coronal chanced to whack his shoulder."
166	   - Agent: Lord Joffrey Caswell
167	   - Patient: Ser Kyle
168	   - Instrument: tourney lance (coronal)
169	   - Location: the lists
170	   - Witness: Dunk
171	   - Outcome: Kyle deliberately falls; his plan to be recognized fails
172	5. **Dunk arms for his tilt** — dons the greathelm and takes up the hanged-man shield and lance; Egg twice urges him to withdraw.
173	   - Agent: Dunk (with Egg attending)
174	   - Location: the south end of the lists
175	   - Outcome: Dunk rides out despite the warnings
176	6. **Ser Uthor Underleaf unhorses Dunk** — at ten yards Uthor lifts his point; "Uthor's iron fist took him square between his eyes, with all the force of man and horse behind it."
177	   - Agent: Ser Uthor Underleaf
178	   - Patient: Dunk
179	   - Instrument: tourney lance (iron-fist coronal)
180	   - Location: the lists
181	   - Outcome: Dunk knocked senseless from Thunder; forfeits horse, arms, and armor
182	7. **The maester tends Dunk for four hours** — examines his eyes, gives a potion; reports Lord Smallwood's nephew's broken wrist and Ser Eden Risley's crushed leg, "but no one has been killed."
183	   - Agent: the maester
184	   - Patient: Dunk
185	   - Location: the cellar
186	   - Outcome: Dunk revives, learns he was unhorsed and his gear is lost
187	8. **Ser Uthor wins his second tilt against Ser Addam Frey** — "Against Ser Addam Frey, a cousin to the bride ... Indeed he did"; "Her Ladyship fainted when Ser Addam fell."
188	   - Agent: Ser Uthor Underleaf
189	   - Patient: Ser Addam Frey
190	   - Location: the lists
191	   - Outcome: Addam Frey unhorsed; Lady Butterwell faints and is helped away
192	9. **Glendon Ball unhorses Lord Alyn Cockshaw** — "plump Lord Alyn Cockshaw was limping off between two squires, the latest conquest of young Glendon Ball," his helm's three feathers broken.
193	   - Agent: Glendon Ball
194	   - Patient: Lord Alyn Cockshaw
195	   - Location: the field
196	   - Outcome: Cockshaw unhorsed and limping
197	10. **The Fiddler unhorses Ser Franklyn Frey** — "Franklyn Frey was down"; "The Fiddler had dismounted, to help his fallen foe back to his feet."
198	    - Agent: Ser John the Fiddler
199	    - Patient: Ser Franklyn Frey
200	    - Instrument: tourney lance
201	    - Location: the field
202	    - Outcome: Franklyn unhorsed; Fiddler advances toward the dragon's egg
203	11. **The dwarfs prepare to leave Whitewalls** — hitching ponies to their wheeled wooden pig and a second wayn near the postern gate; the ugliest glares at Dunk.
204	    - Agent: the dwarfs
205	    - Location: the postern gate
206	    - Outcome: departure underway
207	12. **Maynard Plumm urges Dunk to flee; Dunk refuses** — Plumm: "Take your horse and armor and slip out the postern gate." Dunk: "Even a hedge knight has his honor" — and his gear now belongs to Uthor anyway.
208	    - Agent: Ser Maynard Plumm (urging); Dunk (refusing)
209	    - Location: the well
210	    - Outcome: Dunk stays

258	- **Emotional state:** Hungover and heat-sick, head "beating like a drum"; overconfident before the tilt, then stunned, humiliated, and despairing after ("I only wish I were [dead]"); self-lacerating ("Dunk the lunk"); tempted by flight but resolved on honor
259	- **Primary preoccupation:** Winning his tilts to stay a knight ("Let me win this tilt and one more, and I will be content") — then, after losing, the loss of Thunder, arms, and armor and what it means for his knighthood
260	- **Key decisions made:** Rode the tilt despite Egg's warnings; refused Maynard Plumm's urging to flee and steal away his forfeit horse and gear — "Better a beggar than a thief"
261	- **Self-deception flags:** Dismissed Ser Uthor for his snail sigil ("How quick can he be?"); told himself he had "trained" though "Not so faithfully as he might have"; hoped Uthor would win the whole tourney so he could tell himself "he had lost to the best knight in the field"

# TMK — Dunk 1

## Chapter Metadata
- **book:** TMK
- **chapter_number:** 1
- **pov_character:** Dunk
- **pov_chapter_number:** Dunk I
- **first_available:** pre-agot
- **location_primary:** Whitewalls (Lord Butterwell's castle — the tourney yard/lists, the wine cellar where Dunk is treated, the outer ward, the Milkhouse, and the well)
- **location_secondary:** The cellar/wine-store beneath the castle; the postern gate; (mentioned) Nunny, Bitterbridge, Misty Moor, the Twins / the Crossing, Ashford Meadow, the Reach, Winterfell, Summerhall, Asshai by the Shadow, Flea Bottom, Dorne, Pennytree, the Seven Kingdoms; (recalled) the ship White Lady
- **approximate_timeline:** Tournament morning, the day after the wedding feast; unit part 5 of 9. Sequence: the first-round jousts play out under punishing heat (Paege/Frey, Ball/Argrave, Caswell/Kyle); Dunk arms himself, dismisses his opponent, and is knocked senseless by Ser Uthor Underleaf's lance; he wakes four hours later in the cellar under a maester's care; he returns to the field to watch the Fiddler unhorse Franklyn Frey, passes the departing dwarfs, and at the well is urged by Maynard Plumm to flee Whitewalls, which he refuses.
- **time_markers:** "The sun rose hot and hard"; "this morning"; "last night" (the feast); "an hour before they called him forth again"; "Two more before my turn"; "You have not ridden in a tilt since Ashford Meadow"; "Ser Uthor is ten years my senior and half my size"; a "weedy youth of twenty" (Lord Caswell); "Four hours, by the sundial" (Dunk unconscious); tale of a knight who "slept for forty years"; "before the day is out"; "clouds ... gathering to the east"
- **chapter_summary:** On a blistering tournament morning at Whitewalls, Dunk watches the early tilts — Franklyn Frey unhorsing Harbert Paege, Glendon Ball unhorsing Ser Argrave, and Lord Joffrey Caswell toppling Ser Kyle the Cat, who threw the match hoping to be taken back into Caswell's service. Overconfident and hungover, Dunk dismisses his own opponent, the snail-sigiled Ser Uthor Underleaf, only to be struck square between the eyes by Uthor's lance and knocked from Thunder. He wakes four hours later in a wine cellar, tended by a maester, and learns he has lost his horse, arms, and armor to Uthor by the terms of the tilt. Returning dazed to the field, he sees the Fiddler unhorse Franklyn Frey and pass the departing troupe of dwarfs. At the well, Ser Maynard Plumm urges him to take his horse and armor and slip out the postern gate, but Dunk — his mount and gear now belonging to Uthor — refuses to leave a beggar or a thief, invoking the honor Ser Arlan taught him.
- **unit_part:** 5 of 9

## Physical Environment
- **Weather:** Punishing heat — "The sun rose hot and hard, implacable"; "Waves of heat rose shimmering off the white stones"; no wind, "no breath of wind stirred the banners that drooped"; the sun "beat down on his head like a hammer on an anvil"; a few clouds "gathering to the east, dark as Dunk's mood"
- **Season indicators:** High summer / drought — "baked earth and torn grass," "Clods of cracked dry earth sprayed back from beneath their horses' hooves," "too hot for man or mount"
- **Time of day:** Morning through midday; the sun rising then beating down; "Four hours, by the sundial" pass while Dunk is unconscious
- **Lighting:** Bright, glaring sun off the white stones; the cellar is dim and cool ("At least it is cool here"); "the bright blue sky"
- **Sounds:** The crack and explosion of breaking lances; a cheer "short and thin and hollow"; a trumpet "loud enough to make Dunk wince"; "a noise ... part roar and part moan" when a rider falls; a "gale of laughter" at the herald's mockery; cheering "all around him"; Dunk's voice echoing off the cellar ceiling; his head "beating like a drum," skull "pounding like some monstrous war drum"
- **Smells:** The greathelm lining "stank of old sweat"; the ugliest dwarf "smelled like a privy"; (recalled) baked earth
- **Notable sensory details:** Heat-shimmer off white stone; the drooping green, white and yellow banners; the taste of blood in Dunk's mouth on waking; the "foul potion"; the barrel-vaulted cellar ceiling and casks of wine and ale; a hawk/herald soaring in "wide circles" that Dunk envies

## Events & Actions
1. **The early tilts run under the heat** — Paege and Frey break four pairs of lances before a hollow cheer; the field bakes.
   - Agent: Ser Harbert Paege; Ser Franklyn Frey
   - Location: the lists at Whitewalls
   - Outcome: repeated broken lances, no unhorsing (yet)
2. **Ser Franklyn Frey unhorses Ser Harbert Paege** — after many passes, "the golden horse trotting riderless ... as Ser Harbert Paege rolled feebly on the ground."
   - Agent: Ser Franklyn Frey
   - Patient: Ser Harbert Paege
   - Instrument: tourney lance
   - Location: the lists
   - Outcome: Paege unhorsed
3. **Glendon Ball unhorses Ser Argrave the Defiant** — "Ball's coronal found the center of his foe's breastplate with such force that it burst his saddle cinch. Knight and saddle both went tumbling to the dust."
   - Agent: Glendon Ball
   - Patient: Ser Argrave the Defiant
   - Instrument: tourney lance (coronal)
   - Location: the lists
   - Witness: Dunk ("Dunk was impressed despite himself")
   - Outcome: Argrave unhorsed, saddle burst
4. **Lord Joffrey Caswell unhorses Ser Kyle the Cat** — Kyle throws the match; "the Cat went tumbling when Lord Joffrey's coronal chanced to whack his shoulder."
   - Agent: Lord Joffrey Caswell
   - Patient: Ser Kyle
   - Instrument: tourney lance (coronal)
   - Location: the lists
   - Witness: Dunk
   - Outcome: Kyle deliberately falls; his plan to be recognized fails
5. **Dunk arms for his tilt** — dons the greathelm and takes up the hanged-man shield and lance; Egg twice urges him to withdraw.
   - Agent: Dunk (with Egg attending)
   - Location: the south end of the lists
   - Outcome: Dunk rides out despite the warnings
6. **Ser Uthor Underleaf unhorses Dunk** — at ten yards Uthor lifts his point; "Uthor's iron fist took him square between his eyes, with all the force of man and horse behind it."
   - Agent: Ser Uthor Underleaf
   - Patient: Dunk
   - Instrument: tourney lance (iron-fist coronal)
   - Location: the lists
   - Outcome: Dunk knocked senseless from Thunder; forfeits horse, arms, and armor
7. **The maester tends Dunk for four hours** — examines his eyes, gives a potion; reports Lord Smallwood's nephew's broken wrist and Ser Eden Risley's crushed leg, "but no one has been killed."
   - Agent: the maester
   - Patient: Dunk
   - Location: the cellar
   - Outcome: Dunk revives, learns he was unhorsed and his gear is lost
8. **Ser Uthor wins his second tilt against Ser Addam Frey** — "Against Ser Addam Frey, a cousin to the bride ... Indeed he did"; "Her Ladyship fainted when Ser Addam fell."
   - Agent: Ser Uthor Underleaf
   - Patient: Ser Addam Frey
   - Location: the lists
   - Outcome: Addam Frey unhorsed; Lady Butterwell faints and is helped away
9. **Glendon Ball unhorses Lord Alyn Cockshaw** — "plump Lord Alyn Cockshaw was limping off between two squires, the latest conquest of young Glendon Ball," his helm's three feathers broken.
   - Agent: Glendon Ball
   - Patient: Lord Alyn Cockshaw
   - Location: the field
   - Outcome: Cockshaw unhorsed and limping
10. **The Fiddler unhorses Ser Franklyn Frey** — "Franklyn Frey was down"; "The Fiddler had dismounted, to help his fallen foe back to his feet."
    - Agent: Ser John the Fiddler
    - Patient: Ser Franklyn Frey
    - Instrument: tourney lance
    - Location: the field
    - Outcome: Franklyn unhorsed; Fiddler advances toward the dragon's egg
11. **The dwarfs prepare to leave Whitewalls** — hitching ponies to their wheeled wooden pig and a second wayn near the postern gate; the ugliest glares at Dunk.
    - Agent: the dwarfs
    - Location: the postern gate
    - Outcome: departure underway
12. **Maynard Plumm urges Dunk to flee; Dunk refuses** — Plumm: "Take your horse and armor and slip out the postern gate." Dunk: "Even a hedge knight has his honor" — and his gear now belongs to Uthor anyway.
    - Agent: Ser Maynard Plumm (urging); Dunk (refusing)
    - Location: the well
    - Outcome: Dunk stays

## POV Character's Internal State
- **Emotional state:** Hungover and heat-sick, head "beating like a drum"; overconfident before the tilt, then stunned, humiliated, and despairing after ("I only wish I were [dead]"); self-lacerating ("Dunk the lunk"); tempted by flight but resolved on honor
- **Primary preoccupation:** Winning his tilts to stay a knight ("Let me win this tilt and one more, and I will be content") — then, after losing, the loss of Thunder, arms, and armor and what it means for his knighthood
- **Key decisions made:** Rode the tilt despite Egg's warnings; refused Maynard Plumm's urging to flee and steal away his forfeit horse and gear — "Better a beggar than a thief"
- **Self-deception flags:** Dismissed Ser Uthor for his snail sigil ("How quick can he be?"); told himself he had "trained" though "Not so faithfully as he might have"; hoped Uthor would win the whole tourney so he could tell himself "he had lost to the best knight in the field"

input.content:
# TMK — Dunk 1

## Chapter Metadata
- **book:** TMK
- **chapter_number:** 1
- **pov_character:** Dunk
- **pov_chapter_number:** Dunk I
- **first_available:** pre-agot
- **location_primary:** The tourney encampment at Whitewalls — Dunk and Egg's tent, then Ser Uthor Underleaf's tent
- **location_secondary:** (mentioned) Summerhall, Flea Bottom, Pennytree, the Redgrass Field, Ashford Meadow, Lannisport, Oldtown, King's Landing, the Pussywillows (brothel), across the narrow sea
- **approximate_timeline:** Unit part 6 of 9. Continuous with the tourney day: after his defeat, Dunk returns to his tent, finds Egg brushing Thunder, and refuses Egg's offers to save him. He takes his forfeit horse and armor to Ser Uthor's tent, where a coin-lesson exposes a Daemon Blackfyre gold piece, Uthor lays out his tourney-fixing scheme and Glendon Ball's true parentage, offers Dunk paid employment as a professional loser, and finally reveals he was paid to kill Dunk — closing with a veiled warning.
- **time_markers:** "at sunrise" (the gold delivered); "not long after the master of the games nailed up the pairings"; "Half a year ago" (Glendon's knighting); "The night before the battle" (Redgrass Field); "it has been many a year since I last slept beneath a hedge"; "Six dragons tendered in advance, four more promised when you died"
- **chapter_summary:** In his tent Dunk finds Egg brushing Thunder and, resigned to forfeiting his horse, arms, and armor to Ser Uthor Underleaf, refuses the boy's offers of the horse Rain and service at his father's household in Summerhall, even proposing they part ways. Inside Ser Uthor's richly appointed tent, Dunk examines a gold coin and identifies it as a Daemon Blackfyre piece — "traitor's gold." Ser Uthor, out of armor more merchant than knight, recounts his profitable habit of throwing tourney finals, bribing the master of the games Cosgrove to fix pairings, and exposes Ser Glendon Ball as the whore-born son of Penny Jenny, knighted by Ser Morgan Dunstable in a bargain for his sister's maidenhead. He offers to lend Dunk his gear back if Dunk will travel with him to be unhorsed for show, then reveals he was paid — six dragons in advance, four more on Dunk's death — to strike Dunk's head, the gold delivered by a hooded serving man whose master he does not name. He ties the danger to Dunk having caused the Trial of Seven at Ashford Meadow and the death of Baelor Breakspear, and to the exiled Bright Prince's friends.
- **unit_part:** 6 of 9

## Physical Environment
- **Weather:** —
- **Season indicators:** —
- **Time of day:** Daytime, following Dunk's tilt; a reference to the gold being delivered "at sunrise" that morning
- **Lighting:** Ser Uthor's tent lit within; an iron brazier burning
- **Sounds:** The camp chair creaking "alarmingly" beneath Dunk's weight; a coin bouncing off Will's fingers and falling to the ground
- **Smells:** Perfumed incense burning in the iron brazier inside Ser Uthor's tent
- **Notable sensory details:** The tent's plain sailcloth exterior versus the rich interior; woven Myrish rugs "rich with color"; the heady gold wine; the solid-silver wine cup inlaid with golden snails; the gold coin tasted and hefted in the palm

## Characters Present
| Character | Role in Chapter | First Appearance? | Notes |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------|-------|
| Dunk (Ser Duncan the Tall) | POV; forfeits his gear, hears Ser Uthor's schemes and the assassination reveal | (uncertain — verify) | Uncommon large; competed as the Gallows Knight; still shaken from the head-blow ("felt dizzy again") |
| Egg | Dunk's squire; brushes Thunder, offers his horse and father's household, is refused | (uncertain — verify) | Shaved/bald head; floppy straw hat; brother to Aerion; father keeps a household at Summerhall |
| Ser Uthor Underleaf (the Snail) | Host in his tent; ransoms Dunk's gear, reveals his tourney-fixing and the murder contract | (uncertain — verify) | Out of armor "more a merchant than a knight"; round-shouldered; long fingers; glistening smile |
| Will | Ser Uthor's squire; fails the coin-lesson and is struck and dismissed | Yes | "a gawky fellow close in age to Dunk" |

## Character Appearances
| Character | Hair | Eyes | Build/Height | Scars/Marks | Clothing/Armor | Weapons Worn | Distinguishing Features | Age (if stated) |
|-----------|------|------|-------------|-------------|----------------|-------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Dunk | — | Blow landed "square between his eyes" (recalled) | "uncommon large, Duncan the Tall"; "such a strapping specimen"; "that broad chest" | — | Out of armor; his forfeit mail, greaves, gorget, breastplate, and cracked/dinted helm carried on Thunder | — | Too big to ride a normal-sized man's armor; ducks and stoops through the tent flap | — |
| Egg | Bald/shaved head ("as bald as you") | Eyes "far away" | A boy | Split lip (established) | Floppy straw hat | — | Face "as droopy as his big straw hat" | A boy |
| Ser Uthor Underleaf | — | — | "round-shouldered old man"; "some old man" | — | Out of his armor, "looked more a merchant than a knight" | — | "his lips glistened when he smiled"; "long fingers"; steeples/rubs his chin | Old ("old man") |
| Will | — | — | "a gawky fellow close in age to Dunk" | — | — | — | Fumbles the tossed coin, drops to knees to find it | Close in age to Dunk |

## Characters Referenced
| Character | Context of Reference | Referenced By |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------|
| Ser Arlan of Pennytree | Found Dunk in Flea Bottom chasing pigs; took him as squire; taught him sword, lance, riding; died and was buried by Dunk on a westward hillside | Dunk |
| Ser Arlan's old squire | "His old squire had been slain on the Redgrass Field," creating the vacancy Dunk filled | Dunk |
| Steely Pate | Smith who made Dunk's armor ("Steely Pate made that armor") | Dunk |
| Egg's father | Keeps a household and full stables at Summerhall; Egg offers Dunk service there | Egg |
| Aerion the Bright Prince | Egg's brother; in exile "across the narrow sea"; his friends "will not have forgotten the cause of his exile" | Dunk (internal) / Ser Uthor |
| Baelor Breakspear | Prince "well loved"; died at the Trial of Seven at Ashford Meadow at his brother Maekar's hand | Ser Uthor |
| Maekar | Killed his brother Baelor Breakspear at the Trial of Seven ("at his brother Maekar's hand") | Ser Uthor |
| Daemon Blackfyre | "the Pretender"; struck his own coinage during his rebellion | Ser Uthor |
| King Aerys | Bearded on his coins, "the same as old King Aegon" | Dunk (internal) |
| Old King Aegon | Bearded on his coins | Dunk (internal) |
| Aegon the Unworthy | Named as a benchmark for the coin's age ("not worn enough to be from before Aegon the Unworthy") | Dunk (internal) |
| King Daeron / Daeron the Good | "who'd come between them, had been clean-shaved"; Dunk "knew the face of Daeron the Good, and this wasn't him" | Dunk (internal) |
| Ser Glendon Ball | His purported sire, sham knighting, and whore-born origins recounted; slated to fall to Ser Uthor | Ser Uthor / Dunk |
| Fireball | Glendon's "purported sire"; "had her [Jenny] before that, I don't doubt" | Ser Uthor |
| Penny Jenny / Redgrass Jenny | Camp follower, Glendon's mother; renamed after the Redgrass Field; died | Ser Uthor |
| Glendon Ball's sister | Grew up in the Pussywillows; her maidenhead was the price of Glendon's knighting | Ser Uthor |
| An old squire (near the Pussywillows) | Trained the boy Glendon "in trade for ale and cunt"; could not knight him | Ser Uthor |
| Ser Morgan Dunstable | "clubbed her brother a knight" in the Pussywillows in exchange for the sister's maidenhead | Ser Uthor |
| Kirby Pimm | Claims he witnessed Glendon's knighting; Ser Uthor's source | Ser Uthor |
| Cosgrove | The master of the games, "as fond of silver as the next man"; bribed to fix pairings | Ser Uthor |
| The Old Ox | Slated by Ser Uthor as his next foe ("The Old Ox will be the next to tumble") | Ser Uthor |
| Lord Shawney | "gave five to one, the fool" on the wagering against Ser Uthor | Ser Uthor |
| The Laughing Storm | Named as a knight who "might not agree" that Uthor is the best | Dunk |
| Leo Longthorn | Named as a formidable knight | Dunk |
| The Brute of Bracken | Named as a formidable knight | Dunk |
| Lord Butterwell | Offers "thirty dragons for the knight who comes second" | Ser Uthor |
| The hooded serving man | Brought the gold to Ser Uthor at sunrise, face hooded, "did not speak his master's name" | Ser Uthor |
| The dwarfs | "Perhaps those dwarfs would let me join their troupe" — six dwarfs with pig bladders | Dunk (internal) |

## Locations
| Location | Role | First Appearance? |
|----------|------|-------------------|
| Dunk and Egg's tent | Where Egg brushes Thunder and Dunk resolves to forfeit his gear | Yes |
| Ser Uthor Underleaf's tent | The main setting; plain outside, richly furnished within | Yes |
| Whitewalls | The castle/tourney where all this occurs ("no harm to any man at Whitewalls") | (uncertain — verify) |
| Summerhall | Egg's father's seat; full stables; Egg proposes going there | (uncertain — verify) |
| Flea Bottom | Where Ser Arlan found Dunk chasing pigs | (uncertain — verify) |
| Pennytree | Ser Arlan's home, which Dunk never saw and could not find | (uncertain — verify) |
| The Redgrass Field | Battle where Ser Arlan's old squire was slain and where Penny Jenny was renamed | (uncertain — verify) |
| Ashford Meadow | Site of the Trial of Seven Dunk is said to have caused | (uncertain — verify) |
| Lannisport | Place with smiths who buy old armor; a City Watch that likes big men | (uncertain — verify) |
| Oldtown | A City Watch that likes big men | Yes |
| King's Landing | "from Lannisport to King's Landing"; smiths who buy armor | (uncertain — verify) |
| The Pussywillows | Brothel where Glendon and his sister were raised and where he was knighted | Yes |
| Across the narrow sea | Where the Bright Prince (Aerion) is in exile | Yes |

## Location Descriptions
| Location | Defensive Features | Architecture/Layout | Interior Details | Scale | Condition | Surrounding Terrain/Geography | Notable Sensory Details |
|----------|--------------------|--------------------|-----------------| ------|-----------|------------------------------|------------------------|
| Ser Uthor Underleaf's tent | Staked to the ground with hempen ropes | "a large square box of chin-colored sailcloth"; a center pole bearing a silver snail above a long gray pennon | Ground carpeted in woven Myrish rugs; ornate trestle table surrounded by camp chairs; a feather bed with soft cushions; an iron brazier burning perfumed incense | "large square box" | Very plain outside, luxurious within | The tourney encampment at Whitewalls | Perfumed incense; rich color of the rugs; the plain exterior belying the "comforts" inside |
| Dunk and Egg's tent | — | — | — | — | — | The tourney encampment | Thunder brushed outside it |

## Artifacts & Objects of Significance
| Artifact | Context | First Appearance? | Current Holder/Location |
|----------|---------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Thunder | Dunk's "big brown destrier," brushed by Egg, then forfeit to Ser Uthor and led to his tent laden with arms | (uncertain — verify) | Forfeit to Ser Uthor |
| Dunk's armor (mail, greaves, gorget, breastplate, helm) | Scoured and polished by Egg; helm "cracked and dinted where Ser Uthor's coronal struck"; forfeit | (uncertain — verify) | Forfeit to Ser Uthor |
| The Gallows Knight / hanged-man shield ("his new old shield") | Dunk's device; "The Gallows Knight. What a dismal mystery knight I proved to be"; Uthor mocks it as "hanging ... Dead and defeated" | (uncertain — verify) | Carried on Thunder, forfeit |
| Ser Arlan's sword ("my sword") | Dunk's inherited sword, forfeit with the rest ("My horse and sword are yours") | (uncertain — verify) | Forfeit to Ser Uthor |
| The Daemon Blackfyre gold coin | A gold "dragon" struck with a young clean-shaved king and DAEMON beneath; "Rebel's gold. Traitor's gold"; Uthor will melt it down | Yes | Ser Uthor |
| Ser Uthor's coins (gold dragons, silver stags) | Piles of gold and silver on the table; some clipped, some shaved; his winnings and wagers | Yes | Ser Uthor |
| Ser Uthor's silver wine cup | "solid silver, with a line of golden snails inlaid around the lip" | Yes | Ser Uthor's tent |
| Flagon / cups of wine | Gold-colored wine, "heady on the tongue"; Uthor fills two cups | Yes | Ser Uthor's tent |
| Iron brazier | Burning perfumed incense in the tent | Yes | Ser Uthor's tent |
| Rain | Egg's horse, offered to Dunk ("you could have Rain, ser") | Yes | Egg |
| Maester | A mount Egg would go back to riding ("I'll go back to riding Maester") | Yes | Egg |
| The dragon's egg | The winnings Uthor claims they could buy toward ("win enough to buy a dragon's egg") | (uncertain — verify) | (referenced) |
| Ser Uthor's coronal / lance | The "iron fist" coronal that struck Dunk's head; the weapon he was paid to aim there | (uncertain — verify) | Ser Uthor |
| Egg's floppy straw hat | Swiped by Dunk and snatched back; shades Egg's shaved head | (uncertain — verify) | Egg |

## Food & Drink
| Meal/Occasion | Food Items Described | Drink | Who Is Eating/Drinking | Where | Preparation/Presentation Notes |
|--------------|---------------------|-------|----------------------|-------|-------------------------------|
| Ser Uthor receives Dunk | — | Gold-colored wine, "heady on the tongue" | Dunk (half a cup) and Ser Uthor | Ser Uthor's tent | Poured from a flagon into solid-silver cups inlaid with golden snails; "Half a cup ... You sound in need of it"; Uthor refills his own cup twice |

## Hospitality & Guest Right
| Event | Type | Host | Guest(s) | Details |
|-------|------|------|----------|---------|
| Dunk received in Ser Uthor's tent | Host offering drink to a visitor | Ser Uthor Underleaf | Dunk | "Have a seat ... Will you take wine?"; Dunk first declines, then Uthor insists on "Half a cup"; the amicable hospitality precedes the murder revelation |

## Events & Actions
1. **Egg brushes Thunder; Dunk returns and they reunite** — Egg's eyes "far away," having taken Dunk's fall hard; Egg throws his arms around Dunk ("I knew no stupid snail could kill you, ser").
   - Agent: Egg
   - Patient: Thunder (grooming); Dunk (embrace)
   - Location: Dunk and Egg's tent
   - Outcome: reunion
2. **Dunk refuses Egg's offers and proposes they part** — Egg offers to lend/borrow coin, gives up his horse Rain, and proposes service at his father's Summerhall household; Dunk refuses ("I want no crumbs from your lord father's table") and says "Might be it's time we parted ways."
   - Agent: Dunk
   - Patient: Egg
   - Location: their tent
   - Outcome: Egg refuses to be handed to a proper master-at-arms ("I don't want a proper master-at-arms ... I want you")
3. **Dunk brings Thunder and his arms to Ser Uthor's tent** — Egg leads Thunder (laden with the arms, armor, and the Gallows Knight shield); Dunk tells him to wait outside and stoops through the flap.
   - Agent: Dunk
   - Instrument: Thunder (laden with the forfeit gear)
   - Location: Ser Uthor's tent
   - Outcome: Dunk enters to render the forfeit
4. **Ser Uthor's coin-lesson exposes a Daemon Blackfyre gold piece** — Uthor tosses Will a "good" coin to inspect; Dunk reads DAEMON beneath the king's head and names it; Uthor confirms "the Pretender. Daemon Blackfyre struck his own coinage during his rebellion."
   - Agent: Ser Uthor (setting the test); Dunk (identifying the coin)
   - Patient: Will (the pupil)
   - Instrument: the gold coin
   - Location: Ser Uthor's tent
   - Witness: Dunk
   - Outcome: coin declared "Traitor's gold," to be melted down
5. **Ser Uthor strikes and dismisses Will** — "The Snail clouted him along the side of the head. 'Cretin.' ... He hit the man again. 'Get out of my sight.'"
   - Agent: Ser Uthor
   - Patient: Will
   - Location: Ser Uthor's tent
   - Outcome: Will scrambles from the tent
6. **Uthor questions Dunk's knighthood; Dunk recounts his origins** — Dunk tells how Ser Arlan of Pennytree found him chasing pigs in Flea Bottom, needed a squire after his old one was slain on the Redgrass Field, and taught him arms and riding; and how Dunk buried Arlan facing west.
   - Agent: Dunk (recounting)
   - Patient: Ser Uthor (listener)
   - Location: Ser Uthor's tent
   - Outcome: Uthor advises he "leave out the part about the pigs"
7. **Ransom negotiation over the forfeit** — Uthor refuses to lend the gear back on Dunk's oath or "scratch upon a scrap of paper," demanding "sweet silver ... The coin of the realm."
   - Agent: Ser Uthor; Dunk
   - Location: Ser Uthor's tent
   - Outcome: no ransom agreed
8. **Uthor lays out his tourney-fixing scheme** — He wins semis and throws finals for Butterwell's thirty-dragon second prize plus ransoms and wagers; he bribes Cosgrove, the master of the games, to draw the Old Ox and then Glendon Ball for him.
   - Agent: Ser Uthor
   - Instigator: Ser Uthor (bribing Cosgrove)
   - Location: Ser Uthor's tent
   - Outcome: Dunk realizes the pairings are fixed
9. **Uthor exposes Glendon Ball's parentage and sham knighting** — Born of the camp follower Penny/Redgrass Jenny; Fireball's paternity doubtful; raised with his sister in the Pussywillows; knighted by Ser Morgan Dunstable in the brothel before twenty witnesses in exchange for the sister's maidenhead.
   - Agent: Ser Uthor (recounting)
   - Patient: Dunk (listener)
   - Location: Ser Uthor's tent
   - Outcome: Dunk protests "That's just a tale"; Uthor cites Kirby Pimm as witness
10. **Uthor offers Dunk paid employment as a professional loser** — Lend back steed and armor if Dunk travels with him to be unhorsed in "twenty" more tourneys for a tenth of the winnings; suggests Dunk adopt a fiercer device and grow out hair and beard.
    - Agent: Ser Uthor
    - Patient: Dunk
    - Location: Ser Uthor's tent
    - Outcome: Dunk refuses ("I lost my armor, not my honor")
11. **Uthor reveals he was paid to kill Dunk** — He struck the head deliberately as the mortal target: "I was paid to strike you there" — six dragons in advance, four more promised on Dunk's death.
    - Agent: Ser Uthor
    - Patient: Dunk
    - Instrument: the tourney lance / iron-fist coronal
    - Instigator: an unnamed master (via the hooded serving man)
    - Outcome: Dunk survived; the attempt now disclosed
12. **Uthor names the payment's delivery and the likely cause of the enmity** — A hooded serving man brought the gold at sunrise, unnamed master; Uthor ties Dunk's danger to his having caused the Trial of Seven at Ashford Meadow, Baelor Breakspear's death at Maekar's hand, and the exiled Bright Prince's friends. Closes: "if you dance with dragons, you must expect to burn."
    - Agent: Ser Uthor (revealing)
    - Patient: Dunk
    - Location: Ser Uthor's tent
    - Outcome: Dunk shaken ("a cold hand on his heart")

- **Emotional state:** Humbled and ashamed (owes Egg an apology for scorning the "traitor's tourney" warning); needled to anger by Uthor's arrogance ("Dunk wanted to smash that smile off his face"); then dizzy and frightened at the murder reveal ("felt dizzy again"; "a cold hand on his heart")

- **Key decisions made:** Refused Egg's horse Rain and service at Summerhall; proposed parting ways with Egg; refused Ser Uthor's service offer and the fiercer new device; demanded to know who paid for his death

# TMK — Dunk 1

## Chapter Metadata
- **book:** TMK
- **chapter_number:** 1
- **pov_character:** Dunk
- **pov_chapter_number:** Dunk I
- **first_available:** pre-agot
- **location_primary:** The tourney encampment at Whitewalls — Dunk and Egg's tent, then Ser Uthor Underleaf's tent
- **location_secondary:** (mentioned) Summerhall, Flea Bottom, Pennytree, the Redgrass Field, Ashford Meadow, Lannisport, Oldtown, King's Landing, the Pussywillows (brothel), across the narrow sea
- **approximate_timeline:** Unit part 6 of 9. Continuous with the tourney day: after his defeat, Dunk returns to his tent, finds Egg brushing Thunder, and refuses Egg's offers to save him. He takes his forfeit horse and armor to Ser Uthor's tent, where a coin-lesson exposes a Daemon Blackfyre gold piece, Uthor lays out his tourney-fixing scheme and Glendon Ball's true parentage, offers Dunk paid employment as a professional loser, and finally reveals he was paid to kill Dunk — closing with a veiled warning.
- **time_markers:** "at sunrise" (the gold delivered); "not long after the master of the games nailed up the pairings"; "Half a year ago" (Glendon's knighting); "The night before the battle" (Redgrass Field); "it has been many a year since I last slept beneath a hedge"; "Six dragons tendered in advance, four more promised when you died"
- **chapter_summary:** In his tent Dunk finds Egg brushing Thunder and, resigned to forfeiting his horse, arms, and armor to Ser Uthor Underleaf, refuses the boy's offers of the horse Rain and service at his father's household in Summerhall, even proposing they part ways. Inside Ser Uthor's richly appointed tent, Dunk examines a gold coin and identifies it as a Daemon Blackfyre piece — "traitor's gold." Ser Uthor, out of armor more merchant than knight, recounts his profitable habit of throwing tourney finals, bribing the master of the games Cosgrove to fix pairings, and exposes Ser Glendon Ball as the whore-born son of Penny Jenny, knighted by Ser Morgan Dunstable in a bargain for his sister's maidenhead. He offers to lend Dunk his gear back if Dunk will travel with him to be unhorsed for show, then reveals he was paid — six dragons in advance, four more on Dunk's death — to strike Dunk's head, the gold delivered by a hooded serving man whose master he does not name. He ties the danger to Dunk having caused the Trial of Seven at Ashford Meadow and the death of Baelor Breakspear, and to the exiled Bright Prince's friends.
- **unit_part:** 6 of 9

## Physical Environment
- **Weather:** —
- **Season indicators:** —
- **Time of day:** Daytime, following Dunk's tilt; a reference to the gold being delivered "at sunrise" that morning
- **Lighting:** Ser Uthor's tent lit within; an iron brazier burning
- **Sounds:** The camp chair creaking "alarmingly" beneath Dunk's weight; a coin bouncing off Will's fingers and falling to the ground
- **Smells:** Perfumed incense burning in the iron brazier inside Ser Uthor's tent
- **Notable sensory details:** The tent's plain sailcloth exterior versus the rich interior; woven Myrish rugs "rich with color"; the heady gold wine; the solid-silver wine cup inlaid with golden snails; the gold coin tasted and hefted in the palm

## Hospitality & Guest Right
| Event | Type | Host | Guest(s) | Details |

## Events & Actions
1. **Egg brushes Thunder; Dunk returns and they reunite** — Egg's eyes "far away," having taken Dunk's fall hard; Egg throws his arms around Dunk ("I knew no stupid snail could kill you, ser").
   - Agent: Egg
   - Patient: Thunder (grooming); Dunk (embrace)
   - Location: Dunk and Egg's tent
   - Outcome: reunion
2. **Dunk refuses Egg's offers and proposes they part** — Egg offers to lend/borrow coin, gives up his horse Rain, and proposes service at his father's Summerhall household; Dunk refuses ("I want no crumbs from your lord father's table") and says "Might be it's time we parted ways."
   - Agent: Dunk
   - Patient: Egg
   - Location: their tent
   - Outcome: Egg refuses to be handed to a proper master-at-arms ("I don't want a proper master-at-arms ... I want you")
3. **Dunk brings Thunder and his arms to Ser Uthor's tent** — Egg leads Thunder (laden with the arms, armor, and the Gallows Knight shield); Dunk tells him to wait outside and stoops through the flap.
   - Agent: Dunk
   - Instrument: Thunder (laden with the forfeit gear)
   - Location: Ser Uthor's tent
   - Outcome: Dunk enters to render the forfeit
4. **Ser Uthor's coin-lesson exposes a Daemon Blackfyre gold piece** — Uthor tosses Will a "good" coin to inspect; Dunk reads DAEMON beneath the king's head and names it; Uthor confirms "the Pretender. Daemon Blackfyre struck his own coinage during his rebellion."
   - Agent: Ser Uthor (setting the test); Dunk (identifying the coin)
   - Patient: Will (the pupil)
   - Instrument: the gold coin
   - Location: Ser Uthor's tent
   - Witness: Dunk
   - Outcome: coin declared "Traitor's gold," to be melted down
5. **Ser Uthor strikes and dismisses Will** — "The Snail clouted him along the side of the head. 'Cretin.' ... He hit the man again. 'Get out of my sight.'"
   - Agent: Ser Uthor
   - Patient: Will
   - Location: Ser Uthor's tent
   - Outcome: Will scrambles from the tent
6. **Uthor questions Dunk's knighthood; Dunk recounts his origins** — Dunk tells how Ser Arlan of Pennytree found him chasing pigs in Flea Bottom, needed a squire after his old one was slain on the Redgrass Field, and taught him arms and riding; and how Dunk buried Arlan facing west.
   - Agent: Dunk (recounting)
   - Patient: Ser Uthor (listener)
   - Location: Ser Uthor's tent
   - Outcome: Uthor advises he "leave out the part about the pigs"
7. **Ransom negotiation over the forfeit** — Uthor refuses to lend the gear back on Dunk's oath or "scratch upon a scrap of paper," demanding "sweet silver ... The coin of the realm."
   - Agent: Ser Uthor; Dunk
   - Location: Ser Uthor's tent
   - Outcome: no ransom agreed
8. **Uthor lays out his tourney-fixing scheme** — He wins semis and throws finals for Butterwell's thirty-dragon second prize plus ransoms and wagers; he bribes Cosgrove, the master of the games, to draw the Old Ox and then Glendon Ball for him.
   - Agent: Ser Uthor
   - Instigator: Ser Uthor (bribing Cosgrove)
   - Location: Ser Uthor's tent
   - Outcome: Dunk realizes the pairings are fixed
9. **Uthor exposes Glendon Ball's parentage and sham knighting** — Born of the camp follower Penny/Redgrass Jenny; Fireball's paternity doubtful; raised with his sister in the Pussywillows; knighted by Ser Morgan Dunstable in the brothel before twenty witnesses in exchange for the sister's maidenhead.
   - Agent: Ser Uthor (recounting)
   - Patient: Dunk (listener)
   - Location: Ser Uthor's tent
   - Outcome: Dunk protests "That's just a tale"; Uthor cites Kirby Pimm as witness
10. **Uthor offers Dunk paid employment as a professional loser** — Lend back steed and armor if Dunk travels with him to be unhorsed in "twenty" more tourneys for a tenth of the winnings; suggests Dunk adopt a fiercer device and grow out hair and beard.
    - Agent: Ser Uthor
    - Patient: Dunk
    - Location: Ser Uthor's tent
    - Outcome: Dunk refuses ("I lost my armor, not my honor")
11. **Uthor reveals he was paid to kill Dunk** — He struck the head deliberately as the mortal target: "I was paid to strike you there" — six dragons in advance, four more promised on Dunk's death.
    - Agent: Ser Uthor
    - Patient: Dunk
    - Instrument: the tourney lance / iron-fist coronal
    - Instigator: an unnamed master (via the hooded serving man)
    - Outcome: Dunk survived; the attempt now disclosed
12. **Uthor names the payment's delivery and the likely cause of the enmity** — A hooded serving man brought the gold at sunrise, unnamed master; Uthor ties Dunk's danger to his having caused the Trial of Seven at Ashford Meadow, Baelor Breakspear's death at Maekar's hand, and the exiled Bright Prince's friends. Closes: "if you dance with dragons, you must expect to burn."
    - Agent: Ser Uthor (revealing)
    - Patient: Dunk
    - Location: Ser Uthor's tent
    - Outcome: Dunk shaken ("a cold hand on his heart")
